course_description:
MATH3303 covers key topics in group theory and ring theory, with a view towards commutative algebra, algebraic number theory and representation theory.

This course covers topics fromﾠ modern algebraﾠ, especially groups and rings.ﾠ This is an essential course for any student wishing to undertake further study in pure mathematics and mathematical physics.

In the School of Mathematics and Physics we are committed to creating an inclusive and empowering learning environment for all students. We value and respect the diverse range of experiences our students bring to their education, and we believe that this diversity is crucial for fostering a rich culture of knowledge sharing and meaningful exploration. We hold both students and staff accountable for actively contributing to the establishment of a respectful and supportive learning environment.

Bullying, harassment, and discrimination in any form are strictly against our principles and againstﾠ UQ Policy, ﾠand will not be tolerated. We have developed aﾠ suite of resources ﾠto assist you in recognising, reporting, and addressing such behaviour. If you have any concerns about your experience in this course, we encourage you to tell a member of the course teaching team, or alternatively contact an SMP Classroom Inclusivity Champion (see Blackboard for contact details). Our Inclusivity Champions are here to listen, to understand your concerns, and to explore potential actions that can be taken to resolve them. Your well-being and a positive learning atmosphere are of utmost importance to us

course_aims: On successfulﾠcompletion of this course students should have a solid understanding of the fundamentals of groups, rings and fields.

learning_outcomes.description: Group theory - After completion of this course students should have a solid understanding of the essentials of modern group theory. Topics covered include the three isomorphism theorems, the notion of solvable and simple groups, the JordanHlder theorem and the extension problem.

Ring theory - After completion of this course students should have a solid understanding of basic ring theory. Topics covered include ideals, the characteristic of a ring, the isomorphism theorems for rings, domains and integral domains, fraction fields and unique factorsation domains.

assessment_summary.title: In-Semester Exam
assessment_summary.due_date: The in-semester exam for this course will be held in week 7 - Wednesday 15 April at 5pm.

assessment_details.title: Assignments
assessment_details.task_description: There will be 3 assignments, each worth 10%. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit using Gradescope.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Solutions will be released after 7 days so that is the maximum extension possible.
See ADDITIONAL ASSESSMENT INFORMATION for extension/deferral information relating to this assessment item.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Solutions for assignments will be released 7 days after the assessment is due and as such, late submission after 7 days from the original time submission is due will not be possible, regardless of approved extensions.

assessment_details.title: In-Semester Exam
assessment_details.due_date: The in-semester exam for this course will be held in week 7 - Wednesday 15 April at 5pm.
assessment_details.task_description: The In-Semester exam will be an in-person exam held on campus. The In-Semester exam will be comprehensive, with all material covered in the lectures and applied classes up to week 6 being examinable, unless explicitly noted in the lectures (some more advanced topics and extension material will be excluded). The In-Semester exam will be closed book. No calculators or other aids are allowed. No formula sheets or summary sheets are permitted or provided. learning_activities.topic: Lecture classes Three traditional black/whiteboard lectures per week. Students are expected to actively participate by taking notes, asking questions, and by answering weekly homework problems.
Applied classes During the applied classes students can work on the assignment problems (some of which are deliberately hard and require assistance from the casual academic).

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life.
